input.background: One of the difficult parts about statistical inference is using our imagination to understand the theoretical sampling distributions of an estimator. To exercise our imagination, let us consider the sampling distribution of a hypothetical situation in which two groups, each composed of 1000 individuals, come from populations with risks of 10% for a particular outcome. In studying 1000 individuals per group, the sampling distribution of the risk difference is approximately Normal with expected value 0 and standard deviation.
input.question: Application of the 68-95-99.7 rule allows us to say that 68% of the sample risk difference based in these conditions will fall in the range 0 ± 0.0134, 95% will fall in the range 0 ±(2)(0.0134)=0 ± 0.0268 (and so on).

output.method: 事件发生概率及独立性
output.answer: For example, the probability of observing a risk difference of 0.02 or greater under the stated conditions is Pr(𝑝̂1−𝑝̂2≥0.02)=Pr(Z≥1.49)=0.0681.

group_size.description: The number of individuals in each group.

population_risk.description: The risk of a particular outcome in the population for each group.

risk_difference.description: The difference in risk between the two groups, with an expected value of 0.

standard_deviation.description: The standard deviation of the sampling distribution of the risk difference.

68_percent_range.description: The range in which 68% of the sample risk differences will fall.

95_percent_range.description: The range in which 95% of the sample risk differences will fall.

meta_info.caption: Sampling distribution of a risk difference (Hypothetical).
